planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in moist, well-drained soil in full sun; seeds require warm followed by cold stratification (double dormancy) and typically germinate in their second spring — patience is essential with this species.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun along forest edges, stream banks, or open lowlands; dotted hawthorn is among the showiest of the eastern native hawthorns, producing broad, flat-topped clusters of white flowers in spring that are excellent pollinator forage, followed by heavy crops of round, dull-red to yellow haws distinctively spotted with pale lenticels — the 'dots' that give the species its name. The persistent haws are eagerly consumed by cedar waxwings, robins, grouse, and foxes through fall and early winter, making this one of the most productive wildlife trees in its native range. It forms a dense, thorny canopy well suited to wildlife hedgerows or naturalized areas where its formidable spines can serve as a living fence. Seeds require double dormancy and may take two full seasons to germinate; container-grown transplants establish most reliably.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in sandy, dry, acidic soil in full sun; seeds undergo double dormancy and typically germinate in the second spring — direct sowing at the permanent site is preferred because dwarf hawthorn resents root disturbance.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), infertile soil in full sun; dwarf hawthorn is one of the few native woody plants genuinely adapted to the challenging conditions of pine barrens and sandhills, where richer soils and supplemental moisture will actually harm it by promoting overly lush growth susceptible to disease. Unlike all other native hawthorns, which bear flowers in corymbs, this species bears a single white flower at each thorn tip in spring — an unusual and botanically distinctive habit. The small, round red haws that follow are an important food source for songbirds in the dry-woods habitats where little else fruits abundantly. Its thicket-forming habit makes it useful as a wildlife brush pile and nesting cover in naturalized dry-soil gardens and restorations.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in sandy, dry, acidic soil in full sun; as with most hawthorns, seeds require double dormancy and will germinate in their second spring — sow on-site whenever possible, as transplanting established plants is difficult.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy or rocky, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun along the Coastal Plain; yellow haw is a rare and botanically distinctive native hawthorn prized for its fall display of attractive yellow to greenish-yellow, pear-shaped to rounded haws that are notably sweeter and more palatable than those of most other hawthorns. Its spring flowers provide nectar for early native bees, and the fruit is consumed by mockingbirds, brown thrashers, and other birds that frequent the dry, sandy-woods habitats where this species thrives. It performs best in lean, well-drained soils and is intolerant of heavy clay, standing water, or overly enriched garden beds. Yellow haw's rarity in the wild makes cultivating it from locally sourced seed a valuable contribution to the conservation of Coastal Plain plant diversity.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun near a river bottom or moist woodland edge; double dormancy means germination occurs in the second spring — container transplants from native nurseries are the most practical establishment method.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to occasionally wet soil in full sun along river banks, flood plains, or moist forest margins; littlehip hawthorn is one of the most graceful of the southeastern native hawthorns, with an elegant form, attractive spatula-shaped to shallowly 3-lobed leaves, and masses of tiny (1/4-inch) brilliant red haws that ripen in fall and persist on the branches well into winter. The persistent fruit crop is exceptionally valuable to winter-resident thrushes, cedar waxwings, and other berry-eating birds when larger food sources have been exhausted. White flower clusters in spring provide pollen and nectar for native bees and early butterflies. The tree's smaller stature relative to other hawthorns makes it well suited to suburban wildlife gardens and riparian restoration plantings where a medium-scale fruiting tree is needed.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; seeds require cold stratification and germinate in spring — clumps can also be expanded by transplanting rooted suckers in early spring before bud break.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to light partial shade on dry, rocky upland slopes or brushy hillsides across the northern Great Plains and Great Lakes region; interior serviceberry is a cold-hardy, drought-tolerant clump-former that slowly spreads by root suckers to create a dense thicket of arching stems. White flowers in early spring emerge before the leaves and provide critical nectar for early native bees and overwintered butterflies when few other sources are available. The blue-black berries that ripen in early summer are sweet, flavorful, and high in antioxidants — excellent for fresh eating, jams, and pies, and consumed eagerly by fruit-eating birds before most gardeners can harvest them. It is one of the hardiest serviceberries available, making it a top choice for prairie-edge and windbreak plantings in zones 2–4 where other fruiting shrubs cannot survive.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed directly in fall in sandy, dry, acidic soil in full sun; cold stratification over winter triggers spring germination — established clumps can also be divided in early spring by carefully separating rooted suckers from the parent plant.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Nantucket serviceberry is one of the most compact and low-growing serviceberries in cultivation, native to coastal pine barrens, heathlands, and sandy outwash plains from Cape Cod and Nantucket south through the mid-Atlantic — conditions that most other fruiting shrubs find inhospitable. Its small scale makes it one of the few fruiting native shrubs suited to tight urban lots or coastal cottage gardens where larger serviceberries would overwhelm the space. White flowers appear in early spring and sweet blue-black berries ripen in early summer, attracting catbirds, robins, and warblers. Because it is endemic to a restricted range and specialized habitat, sourcing plants or seed from reputable native plant nurseries rather than wild-collecting is strongly encouraged to protect remaining natural populations.
